critique.summary: The article presents an interactive web experience called 'Web Rewind' created by Opera to explore 30 years of the web from 1995 to 2025. It appears to be a timeline-based journey highlighting web history artifacts, but the provided content is mostly navigational and promotional text without substantive historical information.
mainPoints: Web Rewind is an interactive journey through 30 years of the web.
The timeline covers years from 1995 through 2025.
The experience is made by Opera and includes features like sound effects and voiceovers.
Users can submit memories for a chance to win a trip.
critique.truthfulness: The article does not contain specific factual claims about web history but rather describes an interactive experience created by Opera. Opera is a known web browser company, and it is plausible that they developed such a project. However, the content provided lacks detailed historical information or multiple sources to verify the accuracy of any historical claims.
considerations: The article does not provide detailed historical context or examples from the 30 years of web history.
No external sources or perspectives are cited to support the information.
The interactive experience may require visiting the actual website to assess its educational value and accuracy.
The promotional nature of the content suggests a marketing purpose rather than a comprehensive historical analysis.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the nostalgia and functionality of Opera browser over time, with some users reminiscing about its unique features like mouse gestures and lightweight versions. Others critique the current state of Opera, mentioning its acquisition and commercial practices, while some express confusion or dissatisfaction with the featured web rewind project.

comments.author: al_borland
comments.text:
I have fond memories of Opera. When I migrated off of it to Phoenix, I had a really hard time adjusting to not having mouse gestures. I didn’t know how anyone lived without them.

By the time extensions came around to mimic Opera’s mouse gestures on other browsers, I could never get used to actually using them again.

I was sad to see Opera become just another incarnation of Chrome.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a personal and accurate reflection on Opera's unique features and their impact, showing thoughtful nostalgia without exaggeration.

comments.author: irusensei
comments.text: I remember trying Opera for the first time in Windows 98 SE. It was one of those versions that prided itself for fitting on a floppy. I think it was 3.0.6 or 3.6. But anyway I was taken by surprise how good it was in comparison to Internet Explorer which at the time was the only browser I ever used.
comments.analysis: The comment offers a credible historical perspective on Opera's early versions and their advantages compared to contemporaries.

comments.author: netsharc
comments.text: Feels as soulless as the Opera that's been bought by a Chinese company to sell predatory lending: https://qz.com/africa/1788351/operas-okash-opesas-predatory-...
comments.analysis: This comment references known controversies about Opera's ownership and business practices, adding a critical but relevant viewpoint.

comments.author: ivankra
comments.text:
Eh, marketing fluff.

This is more like it: https://oldweb.today/ - browse old web (from archive.org) with old browsers (in Wasm)
comments.analysis: Provides a useful alternative resource for browsing old web content, contributing constructively to the discussion.

comments.author: Flavius
comments.text: That sure took a lot of work for something that nobody's gonna watch.
comments.analysis: This dismissive comment lacks constructive feedback and appears to be a cynical take without supporting arguments.

comments.author: dag11
comments.text: How do you proceed? I've tried clicking and interacting with everything I can find but I just see the spinning cassette model. Looks cool though!
comments.analysis: Expresses genuine confusion about the interface, which is a valid user experience concern but does not add new information.

comments.author: self_awareness
comments.text:
Erm, how to "use" it?

Or it's just the cassette thing rotating and that's it?
comments.analysis: Similar to the previous comment, it questions the functionality of the project, highlighting possible usability issues.

comments.author: la_oveja
comments.text: is there anything else to it than the cassette 3d thing?
comments.analysis: A brief inquiry about the content, indicating limited engagement but a relevant question about the project's depth.

comments.author: freehorse
comments.text:
In general https://www.web-rewind.com/xywz takes you to year xywz (if exists) but 1999 for some reason takes you to an overview of all years.

edit: https://www.web-rewind.com/1999 would take you to an overview of all years but now it takes you to year 1999
comments.analysis: Provides a factual observation about the website's behavior, useful for understanding navigation quirks.
